questions.stem: The moment of inertia of a system of particles about a fixed axis is defined as which of the following, where $r_i$ is the perpendicular distance of mass $m_i$ from the axis?
questions.explanation: Moment of inertia is the mass-weighted sum of squared perpendicular distances from the axis, $I = \sum_i m_i r_i^2$. The square of the distance is essential, since inertia about an axis grows quadratically with distance. The trap $\sum m_i r_i$ is a first moment, not the moment of inertia.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: Two point masses, $2\ \text{kg}$ at a perpendicular distance $3\ \text{m}$ and $3\ \text{kg}$ at $2\ \text{m}$, rotate about a common axis. What is the total moment of inertia?
questions.explanation: Add each mass times its distance squared: $I = 2(3)^2 + 3(2)^2 = 18 + 12 = 30\ \text{kg m}^2$. Each distance is squared before multiplying by its mass. The trap $12\ \text{kg m}^2$ reports only one of the two contributions.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: The moment of inertia of a uniform thin rod of mass $M$ and length $L$ about an axis through its centre, perpendicular to the rod, is which of the following?
questions.explanation: The standard result for a uniform rod about its centre is $I = \tfrac{1}{12}ML^2$, obtained from $\int_{-L/2}^{L/2} x^2\,(M/L)\,dx$. About one end the value is the larger $\tfrac{1}{3}ML^2$. The trap $\tfrac{1}{3}ML^2$ is the end-axis result, not the central one.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: A uniform thin rod has mass $M = 3\ \text{kg}$ and length $L = 2\ \text{m}$. What is its moment of inertia about an axis through its centre, perpendicular to the rod?
questions.explanation: Using $I = \tfrac{1}{12}ML^2 = \tfrac{1}{12}(3)(2)^2 = \tfrac{1}{12}(3)(4) = 1\ \text{kg m}^2$. The length is squared before applying the $1/12$. The trap $4\ \text{kg m}^2$ is the end-axis value $\tfrac{1}{3}ML^2$, which uses the wrong axis.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: The moment of inertia of a uniform solid disc of mass $M$ and radius $R$ about its central axis, perpendicular to the disc, is which of the following?
questions.explanation: For a uniform disc about its symmetry axis, $I = \tfrac{1}{2}MR^2$. A thin ring of the same mass and radius has the larger $MR^2$ because all its mass sits at the rim. The trap $MR^2$ is the ring value, not the disc value.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: A uniform solid disc has mass $M = 2\ \text{kg}$ and radius $R = 3\ \text{m}$. What is its moment of inertia about its central axis?
questions.explanation: Using $I = \tfrac{1}{2}MR^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}(2)(3)^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}(2)(9) = 9\ \text{kg m}^2$. The radius is squared before the factor of one half. The trap $18\ \text{kg m}^2$ treats the disc as a ring with $I = MR^2$.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: The moment of inertia of a uniform solid sphere of mass $M$ and radius $R$ about a diameter is which of the following?
questions.explanation: For a uniform solid sphere about any diameter, $I = \tfrac{2}{5}MR^2$. A thin spherical shell has the larger $\tfrac{2}{3}MR^2$ because its mass sits farther from the centre. The trap $\tfrac{2}{3}MR^2$ is the hollow-sphere value.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: A uniform solid sphere has mass $M = 5\ \text{kg}$ and radius $R = 2\ \text{m}$. What is its moment of inertia about a diameter?
questions.explanation: Using $I = \tfrac{2}{5}MR^2 = \tfrac{2}{5}(5)(2)^2 = \tfrac{2}{5}(5)(4) = 8\ \text{kg m}^2$. The radius is squared first, then the factor of $2/5$ is applied. The trap $13.3\ \text{kg m}^2$ uses the hollow-sphere factor $2/3$ instead of $2/5$.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: A uniform ring (hoop) has mass $M = 4\ \text{kg}$ and radius $R = 3\ \text{m}$. What is its moment of inertia about the central axis perpendicular to the plane of the ring?
questions.explanation: For a ring all the mass is at radius $R$, so $I = MR^2 = 4(3)^2 = 4(9) = 36\ \text{kg m}^2$. There is no fractional coefficient because every element lies at the same distance. The trap $18\ \text{kg m}^2$ applies the disc factor of one half, which does not belong to a ring.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: The parallel-axis theorem relates the moment of inertia $I$ about an axis to the moment of inertia $I_{\text{cm}}$ about a parallel axis through the centre of mass, a distance $d$ away. Which is the correct statement?
questions.explanation: The parallel-axis theorem adds $Md^2$ to the central moment of inertia: $I = I_{\text{cm}} + Md^2$. The shifted axis always gives a larger moment of inertia, since $Md^2 \ge 0$. The distance $d$ enters squared, matching the dimensions of moment of inertia.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: A uniform rod of mass $M = 3\ \text{kg}$ and length $L = 2\ \text{m}$ has central moment of inertia $I_{\text{cm}} = 1\ \text{kg m}^2$. Using the parallel-axis theorem, what is its moment of inertia about an axis through one end?
questions.explanation: The end is a distance $d = L/2 = 1\ \text{m}$ from the centre, so $I = I_{\text{cm}} + Md^2 = 1 + 3(1)^2 = 4\ \text{kg m}^2$. This matches the direct formula $\tfrac{1}{3}ML^2 = \tfrac{1}{3}(3)(4) = 4\ \text{kg m}^2$. The trap $2\ \text{kg m}^2$ uses $d = L$ wrongly or forgets to square $d$.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: A uniform disc of mass $M = 2\ \text{kg}$ and radius $R = 3\ \text{m}$ has central moment of inertia $\tfrac{1}{2}MR^2 = 9\ \text{kg m}^2$. What is its moment of inertia about a parallel axis tangent to its rim (perpendicular to the disc)?
questions.explanation: The tangent axis is a distance $d = R = 3\ \text{m}$ from the centre, so $I = I_{\text{cm}} + Md^2 = 9 + 2(3)^2 = 9 + 18 = 27\ \text{kg m}^2$. The central value and the shift term add. The trap $18\ \text{kg m}^2$ reports only the shift $Md^2$ and forgets the disc's own $I_{\text{cm}}$.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: The radius of gyration $k$ of a body of mass $M$ with moment of inertia $I$ about an axis is defined by which relation?
questions.explanation: The radius of gyration is the distance at which the whole mass could be concentrated to give the same moment of inertia, so $I = Mk^2$ and $k = \sqrt{I/M}$. It captures how the mass is distributed relative to the axis. The square on $k$ matches the $r^2$ in the definition of $I$.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: A uniform disc has mass $M = 2\ \text{kg}$, radius $R = 3\ \text{m}$, and moment of inertia $9\ \text{kg m}^2$ about its central axis. What is its radius of gyration about that axis?
questions.explanation: From $k = \sqrt{I/M} = \sqrt{9/2} = 3/\sqrt{2} \approx 2.12\ \text{m}$. Equivalently, for a disc $k = R/\sqrt{2}$, which gives the same value. The trap $3\ \text{m}$ is the radius $R$ itself, the radius of gyration only for a ring, not a disc.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: The perpendicular-axis theorem applies to a planar lamina. If $I_x$ and $I_y$ are moments of inertia about two perpendicular axes in the plane of the lamina, and $I_z$ is about the axis perpendicular to the lamina through their intersection, which relation holds?
questions.explanation: For a flat lamina the perpendicular-axis theorem states $I_z = I_x + I_y$, because the squared distance from the perpendicular axis is $x^2 + y^2$. The theorem holds only for planar bodies, where every mass element has zero thickness in $z$. It is the standard route from a diameter moment to the central-axis moment of a disc or ring.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: A uniform disc has central-axis moment of inertia $I_z = \tfrac{1}{2}MR^2$. Using the perpendicular-axis theorem and symmetry, what is its moment of inertia about a diameter?
questions.explanation: By symmetry the two in-plane diameter axes give equal moments, $I_x = I_y$, and the perpendicular-axis theorem gives $I_z = I_x + I_y = 2I_x$. Hence $I_x = \tfrac{1}{2}I_z = \tfrac{1}{2}\cdot\tfrac{1}{2}MR^2 = \tfrac{1}{4}MR^2$. The trap $\tfrac{1}{2}MR^2$ wrongly reuses the central-axis value for the diameter axis.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: A uniform disc has mass $M = 4\ \text{kg}$ and radius $R = 2\ \text{m}$. What is its moment of inertia about a diameter?
questions.explanation: For a disc about a diameter, $I = \tfrac{1}{4}MR^2 = \tfrac{1}{4}(4)(2)^2 = \tfrac{1}{4}(4)(4) = 4\ \text{kg m}^2$. This is half the central-axis value $\tfrac{1}{2}MR^2 = 8\ \text{kg m}^2$, as the perpendicular-axis theorem requires. The trap $8\ \text{kg m}^2$ is the central-axis moment, not the diameter moment.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: Four equal point masses $m$ sit at the corners of a square of side $a$. What is the moment of inertia about an axis through the centre of the square, perpendicular to its plane?
questions.explanation: Each corner is a distance $a/\sqrt{2}$ from the centre, so $r^2 = a^2/2$. With four masses, $I = 4m(a^2/2) = 2ma^2$. The diagonal half-length sets the lever arm, not the side itself. The trap $4ma^2$ wrongly uses $r = a$ for each mass.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: A uniform solid sphere of mass $M = 5\ \text{kg}$ and radius $R = 2\ \text{m}$ has $I_{\text{cm}} = 8\ \text{kg m}^2$ about a diameter. What is its moment of inertia about a tangent line touching its surface?
questions.explanation: A tangent line is a distance $d = R = 2\ \text{m}$ from the centre, so $I = I_{\text{cm}} + Md^2 = 8 + 5(2)^2 = 8 + 20 = 28\ \text{kg m}^2$. This equals $\tfrac{7}{5}MR^2$, the standard tangent result. The trap $20\ \text{kg m}^2$ gives only the shift $Md^2$ and omits the sphere's own moment.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: A uniform ring has mass $M = 2\ \text{kg}$ and radius $R = 3\ \text{m}$. Using the perpendicular-axis theorem, what is its moment of inertia about a diameter?
questions.explanation: The ring's central-axis moment is $I_z = MR^2 = 18\ \text{kg m}^2$, and by symmetry $I_x = I_y$, so $I_z = 2I_x$ gives $I_x = \tfrac{1}{2}MR^2 = 9\ \text{kg m}^2$ about a diameter. The diameter moment is half the central one for a ring. The trap $18\ \text{kg m}^2$ reuses the central-axis value.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: Why does a thin ring have a larger moment of inertia than a uniform disc of the same mass and radius, about the central axis?
options: Because all the ring's mass lies at the maximum distance $R$, while the disc has mass distributed at smaller radii too
Because the ring has more mass than the disc
Because the disc has a hole in the centre
questions.explanation: Moment of inertia weights mass by the square of its distance from the axis, so concentrating all the mass at the rim, as in a ring, maximises the moment of inertia. The disc has much of its mass closer to the axis, lowering its moment to $\tfrac{1}{2}MR^2$ against the ring's $MR^2$. The masses and radii are equal here; only the distribution differs.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: A uniform rod of mass $M$ and length $L$ has central moment of inertia $\tfrac{1}{12}ML^2$. What is its radius of gyration about the central axis?
questions.explanation: From $k = \sqrt{I/M} = \sqrt{\tfrac{1}{12}L^2} = L/\sqrt{12} = L/(2\sqrt{3}) \approx 0.289L$. The mass cancels, leaving a pure multiple of the length. The trap $L/2$ is the half-length, which is the radius of gyration only if all the mass sat at the ends.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: Three point masses lie on a line: $1\ \text{kg}$ at $x = 0$, $2\ \text{kg}$ at $x = 2\ \text{m}$, and $3\ \text{kg}$ at $x = 4\ \text{m}$. What is the moment of inertia about the axis $x = 0$, perpendicular to the line?
questions.explanation: Each distance from $x = 0$ is squared: $I = 1(0)^2 + 2(2)^2 + 3(4)^2 = 0 + 8 + 48 = 56\ \text{kg m}^2$. The far mass at $x = 4$ dominates because of the squaring. The trap $16\ \text{kg m}^2$ keeps only the last term and forgets the $2\ \text{kg}$ contribution.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: A uniform solid disc has mass $M = 2\ \text{kg}$ and radius $R = 3\ \text{m}$. Find its moment of inertia about the central axis and about a parallel axis tangent to its rim.
questions.solution: About the central axis, $I_{\text{cm}} = \tfrac{1}{2}MR^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}(2)(9) = $ {{1}} kilogram metres squared. The tangent axis is a distance $d = R = 3\ \text{m}$ away, so by the parallel-axis theorem $I = I_{\text{cm}} + Md^2 = 9 + 2(9) = $ {{2}} kilogram metres squared.
questions.explanation: The central moment is $\tfrac{1}{2}(2)(9) = 9\ \text{kg m}^2$, and adding the shift $Md^2 = 2(9) = 18\ \text{kg m}^2$ gives $27\ \text{kg m}^2$. The parallel-axis theorem always increases the moment of inertia.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: A uniform thin rod has mass $M = 3\ \text{kg}$ and length $L = 2\ \text{m}$. Find its moment of inertia about a perpendicular axis through the centre and about a perpendicular axis through one end.
questions.solution: About the centre, $I_{\text{cm}} = \tfrac{1}{12}ML^2 = \tfrac{1}{12}(3)(4) = $ {{1}} kilogram metres squared. The end is a distance $d = L/2 = 1\ \text{m}$ away, so $I_{\text{end}} = I_{\text{cm}} + Md^2 = 1 + 3(1) = $ {{2}} kilogram metres squared, which also equals $\tfrac{1}{3}ML^2$.
questions.explanation: The central value is $\tfrac{1}{12}(3)(4) = 1\ \text{kg m}^2$, and the parallel-axis shift gives $1 + 3 = 4\ \text{kg m}^2$, matching $\tfrac{1}{3}(3)(4) = 4\ \text{kg m}^2$. The two routes agree, as they must.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: A uniform solid sphere has mass $M = 5\ \text{kg}$ and radius $R = 2\ \text{m}$. Find its moment of inertia about a diameter and about a tangent line.
questions.solution: About a diameter, $I_{\text{cm}} = \tfrac{2}{5}MR^2 = \tfrac{2}{5}(5)(4) = $ {{1}} kilogram metres squared. A tangent line is a distance $R = 2\ \text{m}$ away, so $I = I_{\text{cm}} + MR^2 = 8 + 5(4) = $ {{2}} kilogram metres squared. The factor in front of $MR^2$ for the tangent result is {{3}} over $5$.
questions.explanation: The diameter moment is $\tfrac{2}{5}(5)(4) = 8\ \text{kg m}^2$, and adding $MR^2 = 20\ \text{kg m}^2$ gives $28\ \text{kg m}^2 = \tfrac{7}{5}MR^2$. The tangent factor is $\tfrac{2}{5} + 1 = \tfrac{7}{5}$.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: Two point masses, $2\ \text{kg}$ at perpendicular distance $3\ \text{m}$ and $3\ \text{kg}$ at perpendicular distance $2\ \text{m}$, rotate about a common axis. Find the total moment of inertia.
questions.solution: The first mass contributes $2 \times 3^2 = $ {{1}} kilogram metres squared, and the second contributes $3 \times 2^2 = $ {{2}} kilogram metres squared. The total moment of inertia is their sum, {{3}} kilogram metres squared.
questions.explanation: The contributions are $2(9) = 18\ \text{kg m}^2$ and $3(4) = 12\ \text{kg m}^2$, giving $I = 30\ \text{kg m}^2$. Each distance is squared before being weighted by its mass.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: A uniform ring has mass $M = 2\ \text{kg}$ and radius $R = 3\ \text{m}$. Find its moment of inertia about the central axis and about a diameter.
questions.solution: About the central axis all the mass is at radius $R$, so $I_z = MR^2 = 2(9) = $ {{1}} kilogram metres squared. By the perpendicular-axis theorem and symmetry, the diameter moment is half of this, $I_x = $ {{2}} kilogram metres squared. The theorem used here is the {{3}} theorem.
questions.explanation: The central-axis moment is $MR^2 = 18\ \text{kg m}^2$, and since $I_z = I_x + I_y = 2I_x$ for the planar ring, the diameter moment is $9\ \text{kg m}^2$. This split between in-plane and perpendicular axes is the perpendicular-axis theorem.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: A uniform disc has mass $M = 2\ \text{kg}$, radius $R = 3\ \text{m}$, and central-axis moment of inertia $9\ \text{kg m}^2$. Find its radius of gyration about that axis.
questions.solution: The radius of gyration satisfies $I = Mk^2$, so $k = \sqrt{I/M} = \sqrt{9/2}$, which is approximately {{1}} metres. For a disc this also equals $R/\sqrt{2}$, confirming that $k$ is {{2}} than the radius $R$.
questions.explanation: Here $k = \sqrt{9/2} = \sqrt{4.5} \approx 2.12\ \text{m}$, equal to $3/\sqrt{2}$. Because the disc has mass nearer the axis than the rim, its radius of gyration is less than $R$.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)
